perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party shall be treated and safeguarded hereunder by the Receiving Party for a period of two (2) years from the date of disclosure. The Receiving Party warrants that it applies reasonable safeguards against the unauthorized disclosure of Proprietary Information and that it will protect such Proprietary Information as least as securely as it protects its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party shall be treated and safeguarded hereunder by the Receiving Party for a period of two (2) years from the date of disclosure. The Receiving Party warrants that it will endeavor to apply safeguards against the unauthorized disclosure of Proprietary Information and that it will attempt to protect such Proprietary Information as securely as it protects its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity by replacing 'applies reasonable safeguards' with 'will endeavor to apply safeguards' and 'will protect such Proprietary Information as least as securely' with 'will attempt to protect such Proprietary Information as securely'. These changes weaken the obligation, making the standard of protection less definitive and more discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the MUTSA, which requires a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. The terms 'endeavor' and 'attempt' suggest a lower standard of care than 'reasonable effort', potentially failing to meet the requirements for trade secret protection under Maryland law. If the Receiving Party only 'attempts' to protect the information, it may not be considered a 'reasonable effort' under MUTSA, thus failing to qualify as a trade secret if disclosed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203 (2022) - Right to Damages :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1203 - Right to Damages Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the standard of care for protecting proprietary information, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'reasonable effort' under trade secret law. The original text states the Receiving Party 'applies reasonable safeguards' and 'will protect such Proprietary Information as least as securely.' The changed text uses 'will endeavor to apply safeguards' and 'will attempt to protect,' lowering the commitment level. Although the provided snippet does not explicitly define 'reasonable effort,' the Law Explanation suggests that 'endeavor' and 'attempt' may not meet this standard, potentially failing to qualify as a trade secret if disclosed. This ambiguity introduces a contradiction as the original text implies a definite standard while the modified text suggests a more discretionary one, potentially undermining the legal protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall have, or shall enter into, agreements with its parent, divisions, subsidiary companies and consultants that will safeguard the Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder consistent with the terms of this Agreement. With respect to employees, the Receiving Party shall advise all employees who will have access to Proprietary Information as to their obligations contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should consider having agreements with its parent, divisions, subsidiary companies and consultants to potentially safeguard the Proprietary Information disclosed hereunder consistent with the general spirit of this Agreement. With respect to employees, the Receiving Party might inform employees who will have access to Proprietary Information as to some of their possible obligations.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces 'shall have' with 'should consider having,' 'will safeguard' with 'to potentially safeguard,' 'consistent with the terms of this Agreement' with 'consistent with the general spirit of this Agreement,' 'shall advise' with 'might inform' and 'obligations contained herein' with 'some of their possible obligations'. This weakens the mandatory requirement for safeguarding and informing parties about obligations, making it discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement focuses on 'Proprietary Information,' the lack of mandatory safeguarding and informing could potentially fail to protect personal information if it is also included within the defined 'Information.' MPIPA requires reasonable security procedures and practices to protect personal information. By making the safeguarding discretionary ('should consider,' 'potentially safeguard,' 'might inform,' 'some of their possible obligations'), the Receiving Party may not be implementing 'reasonable security procedures' as required by MPIPA if the proprietary information also contains personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 14-3503 (2022) - Security Procedures :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 14 - Miscellaneous Consumer Protection Provisions Subtitle 35 - Maryland Personal Information Protection Act Section 14-3503 - Security Procedures Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 14-3503 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) To protect personal information from unauthorized access, use, modification, or disclosure, a business that owns or licenses personal information of an individual residing in the State shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices that are appropriate to the nature of the personal information owned or licensed and the nature and size of the business and its operations. (b) (1) A business that uses a nonaffiliated third party as a service provider to perform services for the business and discloses personal information about an individual residing in the State under a written contract with the third party shall require by contract that the third party implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices that: (i) Are appropriate to the nature of the personal information disclosed to the nonaffiliated third party; and (ii) Are reasonably designed to help protect the personal information from unauthorized access, use, modification, disclosure, or destruction. (2) This subsection shall apply to a written contract that is entered into on or after January 1, 2009. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completenes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the safeguarding of proprietary information and the notification of employees about their obligations, which weakens the mandatory requirements in the original text. The law explanation points to MPIPA, which requires reasonable security procedures to protect personal information. The modified contract uses terms like 'should consider,' 'potentially safeguard,' and 'might inform,' which makes the security procedures discretionary, potentially violating the requirement for 'reasonable security procedures' as mandated by MPIPA if the proprietary information contains personal data. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states in section (a) '...a business that owns or licenses personal information of an individual residing in the State shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices...' This requirement becomes ambiguous due to the changes, creating a legal contradiction by failing to ensure reasonable security procedures are consistently applied, as now it's only discretionary.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that it will not disclose the subject matter or terms of this Agreement or the discussions between the Parties without the written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that it will use reasonable discretion when disclosing the subject matter or terms of this Agreement or the discussions between the Parties, seeking the written consent of the other Party where practical.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'use reasonable discretion' and 'seeking the written consent of the other Party where practical,' the modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The original text imposes a strict prohibition without written consent, while the modified text allows for subjective judgment in certain situations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Contract Law: Enforceability of Contracts
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Maryland Contract Law (various court cases)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a single statute, Maryland contract law emphasizes the importance of clear and unambiguous terms for a contract to be enforceable. Introducing subjective elements like 'reasonable discretion' and 'where practical' creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes about what constitutes a violation of the non-disclosure clause. This ambiguity undermines the enforceability of the contract, as a court may find it difficult to determine whether a party breached the agreement based on these subjective standards.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity to the non-disclosure agreement by adding 'reasonable discretion' and 'where practical,' which weakens the original obligation's strict prohibition. The LLM-generated Law Explanation highlights Maryland contract law's emphasis on clear and unambiguous terms. Although the scraped snippets are unavailable, the Law Explanation asserts that introducing subjective elements like 'reasonable discretion' could lead to disputes and undermine the enforceability of the contract, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: At the Disclosing Party's request, all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party in tangible form that is in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: At the Disclosing Party's request, the Receiving Party will consider whether Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party in tangible form that is in the possession of the Receiving Party should be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces the mandatory 'shall be returned...or destroyed' with the discretionary 'will consider whether...should be returned...or destroyed'. This changes the Receiving Party's obligation from a definite action to a mere consideration, making the return or destruction of proprietary information uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: While MUTSA doesn't explicitly mandate the return or destruction of trade secrets, it implies a duty to protect and prevent unauthorized use. Refusal to return or destroy the information after a request, based on a mere 'consideration,' could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable steps to protect the trade secret, especially if continued possession by the Receiving Party creates a risk of disclosure or misuse. This could support a claim of misappropriation under MUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205 (2022) - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1205 - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1205 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In an action under this subtitle, a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means, which may include granting protective orders in connection with discovery proceedings, holding in-camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return or destruction of proprietary information, while the changed text makes this action discretionary ('will consider whether...should be returned...or destroyed'). The Law Explanation suggests that refusing to return or destroy the information after a request could be interpreted as a failure to protect the trade secret, potentially leading to a claim of misappropriation. Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205 discusses the preservation of trade secrets during legal actions, suggesting a general intent to protect trade secrets. Although the snippet focuses on court actions, the move to make the return of proprietary information only under consideration is a substantial weakening of protection. This is a contradiction because the original text provides a clear directive, while the changed text creates ambiguity regarding the protection of the trade secret.
